steps.title: Map the evidence to what the researchers must have concluded
steps.content:
The passage gives three pieces of data: (1) most sediment is fine-grained mudstone — deposited by low-flow water like a lake; (2) the fine grains show episodic desiccation cracks — the lake periodically dried; (3) intervals of coarse sandstone exist — flowing water (streams) was sometimes present. The blank must account for all three.
  • Choice A — although the area of the Murray Formation experienced a prolonged period of dryness that prevented a lake from forming, water flowing from a distant source was present.: The abundance of mudstone contradicts the idea that no lake formed. This directly conflicts with the evidence. ✗
  • Choice B — a lake existed at the Murray Formation for a prolonged period, though the lake occasionally experienced drying and there were periods in which one or more streams were present.: A prolonged lake explains the dominant mudstone; occasional drying explains the desiccation cracks; periodic streams explain the sandstone intervals. All three evidence points are covered. ✓
  • Choice C — one or more streams existed at the Murray Formation for an extended period until being replaced by a lake that persisted for only a brief period before permanently drying.: A brief lake cannot account for the dominant abundance of mudstone, and the passage never suggests the lake replaced the streams. ✗
  • Choice D — a stream-fed lake was present at the Murray Formation for an extended period, and although the streams experienced occasional drying, the lake did not.: The passage explicitly states the fine grains (the lake sediment) show desiccation cracks, meaning the lake did dry out at times. This choice contradicts that evidence. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the conclusion
steps.content:
The correct statement accounts for all three sediment signals: dominant mudstone (prolonged lake), desiccation cracks (occasional drying), and sandstone intervals (periodic streams).

That confirms B is correct.
